questions.question_text: According to Bohr’s theory, the angular momentum of an electron in 5th orbit is:
questions.question_html: According to Bohr’s theory, the angular momentum of an electron in 5th orbit is:
questions.explanation: For the electron in 5th orbit, angular momentum = mvr = {tex}\frac{5h}{\pi}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.5h}{\pi}{/tex}

questions.question_text: For an electron in the second orbit of hydrogen, what is the moment of momentum as per the Bohr's model?
questions.question_html: For an electron in the second orbit of hydrogen, what is the moment of momentum as per the Bohr's model?
questions.explanation: As per Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, the angular momentum of the electron in allowed orbits is given by L= nh/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} , n = 1,2,3,... For the second orbit n = 2. L = 2 h/2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = h/ {tex}\pi{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of these statements correctly describe the atomic model according to classical electromagnetic theory?
questions.question_html: Which of these statements correctly describe the atomic model according to classical electromagnetic theory?

options.text: The positive charge and negative electron cancel and no waves are radiated
options.html: The positive charge and negative electron cancel and no waves are radiated

options.text: Electrons do not radiate electromagnetic waves
options.html: Electrons do not radiate electromagnetic waves

options.text: The electrons would spiral inwards and fall into the nucleus
options.html: The electrons would spiral inwards and fall into the nucleus

options.text: The waves emitted by electrons are discrete
options.html: The waves emitted by electrons are discrete

questions.correct_option_text: The electrons would spiral inwards and fall into the nucleus
questions.explanation: The electrons revolve round their permitted orbits with acceleration and continuously radiate energy in the form of electromagnetic waves. Due to this continuous loss of energy, they would spiral inward and eventually fall into the nucleus (Classical theory).

options.text: Some particles would be expected at 75 0
options.html: Some particles would be expected at 75<sup>0</sup>

options.text: All the particles would go through the foil with hardly any deflection
options.html: All the particles would go through the foil with hardly any deflection

options.text: All the particles would bounce from the foil at 180 0
options.html: All the particles would bounce from the foil at 180<sup>0</sup>

options.text: Some particles would be expected at 45 0
options.html: Some particles would be expected at 45<sup>0</sup>

questions.correct_option_text: All the particles would go through the foil with hardly any deflection
questions.explanation: All the alpha particles were expected to pass through the gold foil with very little or no scattering, according to the JJ Thomson's "Plum pudding model".

questions.question_text: The basic force acting on the alpha particles using Coulomb's law is
questions.question_html: The basic force acting on the alpha particles using Coulomb's law is
questions.explanation: Alpha particles have two units of positive charge (2e) and the gold nucleus has a charge of (Ze) where Z is the atomic number of the atom (Z=79). Both the alpha particles and the nucleus are small enough to be considered as point mass. Also as gold nuclues is 50 times heavier than alpha particle, it is assumed to remain stationary during the entire duration of the experiment. Thus by Coulomb's law, the force is {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{(2 e)(Z e)}{r^2}{/tex}

options.html: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were zero at {tex}\phi {/tex} =70{tex}^\circ {/tex}

options.text: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were non zero at all angles including at 180 {tex}^\circ {/tex}
options.html: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were non zero at all angles including at 180{tex}^\circ {/tex}

options.text: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were zero at {tex}\phi {/tex} =80 {tex}^\circ {/tex}
options.html: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were zero at {tex}\phi {/tex} =80{tex}^\circ {/tex}

options.text: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were zero at {tex}\phi {/tex} =180 {tex}^\circ {/tex}
options.html: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were zero at {tex}\phi {/tex} =180{tex}^\circ {/tex}

questions.correct_option_text: Number of particles at angle {tex}\phi {/tex} were non zero at all angles including at 180 {tex}^\circ {/tex}
questions.explanation: Most of the alpha particles were deflected through small angles (at least by 1 degree), some were scattered through larger angles and some rebounced back through angles greater than 90 degrees.

questions.question_text: The model that best explains the results of Geiger-Marsden experiment is
questions.question_html: The model that best explains the results of Geiger-Marsden experiment is
questions.explanation: Ernest Rutherford who was a former research student of JJ Thomson supervised the experiment carried by two of his students Hans Geiger and Ernst Marsden. Rutherford felt that there existed a nucleus at the centre of the atom having positive charge and thus proposed the Rutherford model.

questions.question_text: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, an electron can revolve round a proton indefinitely, if its path is
questions.question_html: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, an electron can revolve round a proton indefinitely, if its path is

options.text: a perfect circle of any radius
options.html: a perfect circle of any radius

options.text: a circle of an allowed radius
options.html: a circle of an allowed radius

options.text: a circle of constantly decreasing radius
options.html: a circle of constantly decreasing radius

questions.correct_option_text: a circle of an allowed radius
questions.explanation: Electrons revolve around the nucleus on a selected circular path only.
The continuous spectrum is obtained from Sun, incandascent solids and red hot gases at high pressure.

questions.correct_option_text: If we pass white (continuous-spectrum) light through a cool gas
questions.explanation: If a gas, liquid or transparent solid substance is placed between a light source giving a continuous spectrum, the substance absorbs certain part of the spectrum giving few dark lines or bands in these areas. This is called absorption spectrum.

questions.question_text: To produce an emission spectrum of hydrogen it needs to be
questions.question_html: To produce an emission spectrum of hydrogen it needs to be

options.text: hot and white light shining through
options.html: hot and white light shining through

options.text: cold and white light shining through
options.html: cold and white light shining through

questions.explanation: The spectrum of light emitted by a luminous source is called emission spectrum of that source. Hydrogen gas when heated to a high temperature, splits up into hydrogen atoms. These atoms initially absorb energy and move on to higher energy levels. After a small duration, they emit different amounts of energy and return back to their older level. This produces large number of lines in the atomic spectrum of hydrogen. Each line corresponds to light of specific wavelengths.
